Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days but then I'd just... not. Most of my 14 hours of studying happened in random bursts, usually when I remembered something was due or when I felt guilty enough. I learn better by listening anyway, so I'd sometimes just play lecture recordings while doing other stuff, which felt productive even if it wasn't super focused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. If nothing was due soon, I'd drift for days without touching course materials. When something was coming up, I'd cram everything into a few intense sessions. It wasn't healthy but it's just how I operated.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and get this scared feeling in my stomach. Sometimes the gamified task notifications would pull me in because they made it feel less overwhelming. But mostly it was that anxious realization that I was falling behind.
Q4: Not really, no. There were brief periods where I'd log in a few days in a row, especially when the gamified elements gave me points or badges for completing things. That made it feel less like a chore. But it never stuck as a real habit - something would come up or I'd just lose momentum and stop logging in for a week.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I watched a lot of them - over 260 minutes worth. Being able to pause and replay helped when concepts weren't clicking. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller chunks that felt achievable rather than this massive overwhelming course load.
Q6: The discussion boards felt pointless to me. I only posted like 10 times the whole course. Reading through everyone's comments felt time-consuming and I wasn't sure what I was supposed to get out of it. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings - they just piled up and became this intimidating backlog I couldn't face.
Q7: If something felt like it would directly help me pass, I'd do it. The gamified tasks were good because completing them gave immediate feedback and that little dopamine hit from earning points. Activities that felt vague or disconnected from actual grades just didn't seem worth the effort when I was already struggling to keep up.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. I was barely keeping up with required work, so anything labeled optional got mentally filed under 'never going to happen.' Required activities at least had consequences attached, which gave me the push I needed. Optional things just couldn't compete for my limited motivation.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or lying in bed. Sometimes I'd screenshot important slides and review them later without being connected to the LMS at all. I also had a notebook where I'd jot down key points from audio lectures, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours with downloaded materials, just listening and re-listening to explanations until they made sense. None of that would show in my activity logs. Or I'd be working through concepts in my head during random moments - on the bus, before sleep. That kind of mental processing is invisible but it's still learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video once, then watch it multiple times offline over the next few days. The LMS would only show one access, but I might have engaged with that content three or four times. It was easier on my data plan and meant I could study without needing wifi.
Q12: Around midterms I hit a wall. I was scared about how much I'd let slide and that fear kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up I'd freeze rather than tackle them. The anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how behind I was.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Seeing incomplete tasks and missed deadlines just made me feel worse. Competing responsibilities pulled my attention away constantly - I don't do extracurriculars but life still gets complicated. Fatigue made everything feel twice as hard.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be scrolling through, clicking things, but not actually absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty without actually learning. Those sessions were basically wasted time.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. Even when I knew something was important, I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow. The fear of doing it badly made me avoid starting at all. It's this weird cycle where the anxiety about the task makes you avoid it, which creates more anxiety.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, cramming everything in. My login patterns probably looked like flatlines punctuated by frantic spikes. It wasn't sustainable but it's how I scraped through.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me. Good feedback on assignments - my scores were actually decent when I submitted - made me feel capable, which helped my mood. But I often didn't check feedback promptly because I was scared of criticism. The gamified task feedback was easier to handle since it was immediate and game-like.
Q18: The course having 15 different online components felt overwhelming from the start. I never felt like I had a handle on everything I was supposed to be doing. The structure didn't guide me enough - too many options, not enough clear pathways. I needed more hand-holding than the course provided, honestly.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier - they gave me small wins and made progress visible. But the sheer volume of content made it harder. Also, not having clear priorities meant I'd waste time on less important things while ignoring crucial assignments. A simpler, more linear structure would have helped someone like me.
Q20: I think that's someone trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're reading and watching everything but can't translate that into completed assignments. Or they're anxious like me - engaging with materials feels safer than submitting work that will be judged. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That sounds familiar, actually. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline - listening to downloaded lectures, reviewing notes. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to absorb material. The LMS is a tool, not the only way to learn.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, which captures some patterns. But they miss everything that happens offline, and they can't tell if someone was actually focused or just had a tab open. They definitely can't capture understanding or the mental work of processing information. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. Honestly, I wasn't very engaged this course - I was mostly just trying to survive and pass. Real engagement would feel less like a chore.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt most of the time, or how that fear both drove me and paralyzed me. It wouldn't capture the relief when gamified tasks made things feel manageable, or the shame when I avoided logging in. My emotional journey through this course was intense but completely invisible in the data.
